item.title: Water exchange process and bulk composition regulate slab dynamics and deep earthquakes
item.dc:creator: Jintao Zhu
Renbiao Tao
Lifei Zhang
Takayuki Ishii
item.description: Water strongly influences deep mantle processes, including geochemical cycles, slab dynamics, and deep-focus earthquakes. Yet the stability and interactions of hydrous minerals with nominally anhydrous minerals (NAMs) under the water-undersaturated conditions of subducting slabs remain unclear. Here we show, using high-pressure and high-temperature experiments on MgO–SiO_2–H_2O systems (~2 wt% H_2O), that hydrous minerals progressively dehydrate in the mantle transition zone, transferring water to NAMs such as wadsleyite and ringwoodite, while the cold slab core stays nearly dry. Rapid dehydration near the top of the lower mantle may generate fluids linked to the deepest earthquakes. Our results indicate that dry olivine transformations, rather than dehydration embrittlement, likely trigger most deep-focus earthquakes, and that hydration variations in NAMs influence slab deformation and stagnation above 660 km. The bulk Mg/Si ratio controls hydrous mineral stability, suggesting harzburgite transports water more efficiently than peridotite. This study shows that minerals within subducting slabs host water unevenly, generating strong hydration gradients through dehydration–hydration reactions in the transition zone. These gradients promote slab deformation and provide a mechanism for the occurrence of deep earthquakes. Published: 2026-07-03T13:26:06+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.752 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jintao Zhu; Renbiao Tao; Lifei Zhang; Takayuki Ishii

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-74842-y

Relevance: 0.752 (flagship)

Water strongly influences deep mantle processes, including geochemical cycles, slab dynamics, and deep-focus earthquakes. Yet the stability and interactions of hydrous minerals with nominally anhydrous minerals (NAMs) under the water-undersaturated conditions of subducting slabs remain unclear. Here we show, using high-pressure and high-temperature experiments on MgO–SiO_2–H_2O systems (~2 wt% H_2O), that hydrous minerals progressively dehydrate in the mantle transition zone, transferring water to NAMs such as wadsleyite and ringwoodite, while the cold slab core stays nearly dry. Rapid dehydration near the top of the lower mantle may generate fluids linked to the deepest earthquakes. Our results indicate that dry olivine transformations, rather than dehydration embrittlement, likely trigger most deep-focus earthquakes, and that hydration variations in NAMs influence slab deformation and stagnation above 660 km. The bulk Mg/Si ratio controls hydrous mineral stability, suggesting harzburgite transports water more efficiently than peridotite. This study shows that minerals within subducting slabs host water unevenly, generating strong hydration gradients through dehydration–hydration reactions in the transition zone. These gradients promote slab deformation and provide a mechanism for the occurrence of deep earthquakes.

item.title: Seismological evidence for fluid accumulation beneath the dormant East Eifel volcanic region, Germany
item.dc:creator: Hao Zhang
Torsten Dahm
Pınar Büyükakpınar
Simone Cesca
Marius Paul Isken
Patrick Laumann
Wolfram Hartmut Geißler
item.description: Abstract The East Eifel region is a Cenozoic-Quaternary intraplate volcanic field, well known as the type locality for maar volcanism. Its most recent major eruption occurred at the Laacher See volcano about 13,000 years ago and is regarded as the largest late Quaternary volcanic eruption in central Europe. Despite its dormant state, ongoing regional mantle-derived CO 2 degassing and persistent microseismicity, particularly within the Ochtendung Seismic Zone, indicate active subsurface processes, likely related to fluid migration. Using the Eifel large-N seismic network, we recorded over 500 local microearthquakes from September 2022 to August 2023. Earthquakes within the Ochtendung Seismic Zone exhibit distinct secondary S-wave phases, indicating strong impedance contrasts associated with fluid accumulations within the crust. By extracting travel-time differences between direct and reflected S waves and applying an eikonal-based reverse-time-migration-like approach, we image localized fluid-rich zones at depths of 12–14 km beneath the microearthquakes, suggesting the accumulation of magmatic fluids aligned near the seismic zone. Microearthquakes within the Ochtendung Seismic Zone occur as burst-like sequences reflecting successive ruptures. We propose that the sustained microseismicity results from slip reactivation induced by transient fluid flux, representing an example of natural hydraulic fracturing. Published: 2026-07-03T03:44:56+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.566 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hao Zhang; Torsten Dahm; Pınar Büyükakpınar; Simone Cesca; Marius Paul Isken; Patrick Laumann; Wolfram Hartmut Geißler

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03778-4

Relevance: 0.566 (flagship)

Abstract The East Eifel region is a Cenozoic-Quaternary intraplate volcanic field, well known as the type locality for maar volcanism. Its most recent major eruption occurred at the Laacher See volcano about 13,000 years ago and is regarded as the largest late Quaternary volcanic eruption in central Europe. Despite its dormant state, ongoing regional mantle-derived CO 2 degassing and persistent microseismicity, particularly within the Ochtendung Seismic Zone, indicate active subsurface processes, likely related to fluid migration. Using the Eifel large-N seismic network, we recorded over 500 local microearthquakes from September 2022 to August 2023. Earthquakes within the Ochtendung Seismic Zone exhibit distinct secondary S-wave phases, indicating strong impedance contrasts associated with fluid accumulations within the crust. By extracting travel-time differences between direct and reflected S waves and applying an eikonal-based reverse-time-migration-like approach, we image localized fluid-rich zones at depths of 12–14 km beneath the microearthquakes, suggesting the accumulation of magmatic fluids aligned near the seismic zone. Microearthquakes within the Ochtendung Seismic Zone occur as burst-like sequences reflecting successive ruptures. We propose that the sustained microseismicity results from slip reactivation induced by transient fluid flux, representing an example of natural hydraulic fracturing.

item.title: Absolute chronology of the Early Palaeolithic Karatau Culture in Central Asia
item.dc:creator: Aske L. Sørensen
Anton A. Anoikin
Kseniya A. Kolobova
Tura U. Khudjageldiev
Petr M. Sosin
Natalia Taratunina
Ekaterina P. Kulakova
Olga Tokareva
Olga Meshcheryakova
Aljasil Chirakkal
Amélie Challier
Ramona Schneider
Asleddin Karayev
Islom Ashurmadov
Abdullo Sharipov
Vladimir Pavlov
Andrei Rybalko
Farhad Khormali
Andrew S. Murray
Gábor Ujvari
Jago J. Birk
Jesper Olsen
Thomas M. Hansen
Redzhep N. Kurbanov
Thomas Stevens
David K. Wright
Jan-Pieter Buylaert
Mads F. Knudsen
item.description: Abstract Central Asia represents a key region for our understanding of early human dispersal patterns, because it served as a migration corridor that linked the Levant and southern Caucasus with Northeast Asia. However, no Early Palaeolithic sites in Central Asia are anchored with reliable age constraints, including the thick loess-palaeosol sections in Tajikistan that recorded early human activities and environmental changes over multiple glacial-interglacial cycles. This lack of absolute age constraints is presently a key factor limiting our understanding of the early human occupation history of this region. Here, we provide a comprehensive description and an absolute chronological framework for the Early Palaeolithic Karatau Culture; defined by the rich lithic assemblages found in palaeosols in the Khovaling Loess Plateau, Tajikistan. Age constraints are provided through multi-method analysis of three loess-palaeosol sections in the Khovaling Loess Plateau, combining luminescence ages, cosmogenic 26 Al- 10 Be concentrations, and magnetostratigraphic boundaries into a probabilistic inverse age-depth model. This model shows that the Karatau Culture flourished with the onset of Marine Isotope Stage 15, thrived during Marine Isotope Stage 13 and 11, but disappeared around onset of Marine Isotope Stage 10. We frame the archaeological occupations within local and regional ecological settings to better understand the drivers of Pleistocene human migrations in Central Asia. Published: 2026-07-01T22:23:18+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.525 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Aske L. Sørensen; Anton A. Anoikin; Kseniya A. Kolobova; Tura U. Khudjageldiev; Petr M. Sosin; Natalia Taratunina; Ekaterina P. Kulakova; Olga Tokareva; Olga Meshcheryakova; Aljasil Chirakkal; Amélie Challier; Ramona Schneider; Asleddin Karayev; Islom Ashurmadov; Abdullo Sharipov; Vladimir Pavlov; Andrei Rybalko; Farhad Khormali; Andrew S. Murray; Gábor Ujvari; Jago J. Birk; Jesper Olsen; Thomas M. Hansen; Redzhep N. Kurbanov; Thomas Stevens; David K. Wright; Jan-Pieter Buylaert; Mads F. Knudsen

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-74938-5

Relevance: 0.525 (flagship)

Abstract Central Asia represents a key region for our understanding of early human dispersal patterns, because it served as a migration corridor that linked the Levant and southern Caucasus with Northeast Asia. However, no Early Palaeolithic sites in Central Asia are anchored with reliable age constraints, including the thick loess-palaeosol sections in Tajikistan that recorded early human activities and environmental changes over multiple glacial-interglacial cycles. This lack of absolute age constraints is presently a key factor limiting our understanding of the early human occupation history of this region. Here, we provide a comprehensive description and an absolute chronological framework for the Early Palaeolithic Karatau Culture; defined by the rich lithic assemblages found in palaeosols in the Khovaling Loess Plateau, Tajikistan. Age constraints are provided through multi-method analysis of three loess-palaeosol sections in the Khovaling Loess Plateau, combining luminescence ages, cosmogenic 26 Al- 10 Be concentrations, and magnetostratigraphic boundaries into a probabilistic inverse age-depth model. This model shows that the Karatau Culture flourished with the onset of Marine Isotope Stage 15, thrived during Marine Isotope Stage 13 and 11, but disappeared around onset of Marine Isotope Stage 10. We frame the archaeological occupations within local and regional ecological settings to better understand the drivers of Pleistocene human migrations in Central Asia.

item.title: Ultralow-angle faults produce giant earthquakes
item.dc:creator: Satoshi Ide
Mikito Furuichi
Dye S. K. Sato
item.description: What controls the occurrence of giant earthquakes remains a central question in seismology. We reveal a notably simple—but previously overlooked—relationship between earthquake size and fault dip in subduction zones. Global earthquake statistics show a robust dependence of earthquake size on fault dip, with the probability of extreme events peaking on ultralow-angle faults. Through three case studies, we demonstrate that earthquakes tend to grow larger when the fault geometry is aligned with the regional stress field. We further discuss how such favorable stress configurations are generated along subduction interfaces and emphasize the critical importance of monitoring the temporal evolution of stress in subduction zones to better forecast future megaearthquakes. Published: 2026-07-01T17:59:22+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.564 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Satoshi Ide; Mikito Furuichi; Dye S. K. Sato

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aee3921

Relevance: 0.564 (flagship)

What controls the occurrence of giant earthquakes remains a central question in seismology. We reveal a notably simple—but previously overlooked—relationship between earthquake size and fault dip in subduction zones. Global earthquake statistics show a robust dependence of earthquake size on fault dip, with the probability of extreme events peaking on ultralow-angle faults. Through three case studies, we demonstrate that earthquakes tend to grow larger when the fault geometry is aligned with the regional stress field. We further discuss how such favorable stress configurations are generated along subduction interfaces and emphasize the critical importance of monitoring the temporal evolution of stress in subduction zones to better forecast future megaearthquakes.

item.title: Earth is mostly diffusive: A global analysis of landscape evolution
item.dc:creator: Harrison K. Martin
Michael P. Lamb
item.description: Earth’s landscapes—from mountains to river deltas—are shaped by sediment erosion, transport, and deposition. However, most landscape evolution models represent only two processes: diffusive soil creep and bedrock river incision. How much of Earth’s surface can be represented by these two processes alone? To address this question, we mapped the global abundance and distribution of eight major geomorphic process domains, finding that ~75% of Earth’s land area consists of soil-mantled hillslopes and flatlands dominated by diffusive soil creep, while 0.5% is a bedrock river corridor. While other process domains (glaciers, lakes, alluvial river corridors, aeolian deserts, and bedrock hillslopes) make up only ~24% of Earth’s land area, they transport most of the sediment from continents to oceans. Process domains align with different topographic, climatic, and tectonic conditions, indicating a pathway to improve coupled global landscape, climate, and tectonic evolution models. Overall, our results reveal the distribution of the eight major geomorphic process domains globally and highlight the spatial dominance of diffusive topography. Published: 2026-07-01T17:59:22+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.504 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Harrison K. Martin; Michael P. Lamb

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aeb5187

Relevance: 0.504 (flagship)

Earth’s landscapes—from mountains to river deltas—are shaped by sediment erosion, transport, and deposition. However, most landscape evolution models represent only two processes: diffusive soil creep and bedrock river incision. How much of Earth’s surface can be represented by these two processes alone? To address this question, we mapped the global abundance and distribution of eight major geomorphic process domains, finding that ~75% of Earth’s land area consists of soil-mantled hillslopes and flatlands dominated by diffusive soil creep, while 0.5% is a bedrock river corridor. While other process domains (glaciers, lakes, alluvial river corridors, aeolian deserts, and bedrock hillslopes) make up only ~24% of Earth’s land area, they transport most of the sediment from continents to oceans. Process domains align with different topographic, climatic, and tectonic conditions, indicating a pathway to improve coupled global landscape, climate, and tectonic evolution models. Overall, our results reveal the distribution of the eight major geomorphic process domains globally and highlight the spatial dominance of diffusive topography.

item.title: Accelerating glacier recession contrasts rock glacier stability in a temperate mountain range
item.dc:creator: Ashlesha Khatiwada
Daniel McGrath
Justin Pomeranz
Gordan Gianniny
Lusha Tronstad
Debra Finn
Elizabeth Case
Friedrich Knuth
Darren Larsen
Simeon Caskey
Madeline Grubb
Scott Hotaling
item.description: Cryospheric landforms play a critical role in alpine hydrology and ecosystems. Using historical and contemporary data spanning nearly six decades (1967–2024), we assessed elevation change for glaciers, rock glaciers, and perennial snowfields and the thermal response of streams in the Teton Range, Wyoming, United States. Glaciers and snowfields thinned at −0.84 ± 0.07 meters per year (m year −1 ) and −0.59 ± 0.04 m year −1 between 2014 and 2022, a ~7-fold increase relative to 1967–2014, driven by warming summer temperatures. In contrast, rock glaciers are near equilibrium (−0.05 ± 0.05 m year −1 ) and saw no change in rate. Since 2015, snowfield-fed streams have warmed rapidly (+3.4°C), whereas glacier- and rock glacier–fed streams have warmed at lower magnitudes (+0.9° and +0.6°C, respectively). Our results demonstrate the greater resilience of rock glaciers to atmospheric warming, highlighting the critical role that these features will play as glaciers and perennial snowfields are lost. Published: 2026-07-01T17:59:22+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.475 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ashlesha Khatiwada; Daniel McGrath; Justin Pomeranz; Gordan Gianniny; Lusha Tronstad; Debra Finn; Elizabeth Case; Friedrich Knuth; Darren Larsen; Simeon Caskey; Madeline Grubb; Scott Hotaling

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aee5865

Relevance: 0.475 (flagship)

Cryospheric landforms play a critical role in alpine hydrology and ecosystems. Using historical and contemporary data spanning nearly six decades (1967–2024), we assessed elevation change for glaciers, rock glaciers, and perennial snowfields and the thermal response of streams in the Teton Range, Wyoming, United States. Glaciers and snowfields thinned at −0.84 ± 0.07 meters per year (m year −1 ) and −0.59 ± 0.04 m year −1 between 2014 and 2022, a ~7-fold increase relative to 1967–2014, driven by warming summer temperatures. In contrast, rock glaciers are near equilibrium (−0.05 ± 0.05 m year −1 ) and saw no change in rate. Since 2015, snowfield-fed streams have warmed rapidly (+3.4°C), whereas glacier- and rock glacier–fed streams have warmed at lower magnitudes (+0.9° and +0.6°C, respectively). Our results demonstrate the greater resilience of rock glaciers to atmospheric warming, highlighting the critical role that these features will play as glaciers and perennial snowfields are lost.

item.title: Measurement of isotope fractionation associated with crystal nucleation: Implications for biocrystallization studies
item.dc:creator: Chuan Liu
Thomas L. Goût
Yandi Hu
Weiqiang Li
Shichao An
Zehao Zhou
Song Cai
Huazhang Zhao
Jinren Ni
James J. De Yoreo
Donald J. DePaolo
item.prism:publicationName: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
item.description: Isotopic fractionation is a unique indicator of the mechanisms of mineral precipitation from aqueous solutions, but existing theory does not account for nucleation effects or nonclassical growth mechanisms. Here, using barite (BaSO 4 ) as a model crystal, we provide an experimental measurement of isotope fractionation associated with nucleation. We isolated nucleation effects by precipitating crystals on an organic film, where cation enrichment creates a highly supersaturated microenvironment so nucleation dominates over crystal growth. In a single batch experiment, we retrieved nanocrystals (~1.5 nm) from the organic substrate and large microcrystals in bulk solution where growth dominates. The 138 Ba/ 134 Ba isotopic fractionation of −0.6 to −0.8‰ for the microcrystals match those expected for classical ion-by-ion growth from a moderately supersaturated solution, whereas the fractionation for the nucleation-dominated nanocrystals is about −0.1‰, falling within the reported equilibrium fractionation range between barite and aqueous Ba 2+ . These results demonstrate that cation isotopic fractionation in sparingly soluble salts like barite and calcite is not a single-valued function of saturation indices (SI) and precipitation rate as predicted with current theory, with the fractionation factor (∆ 138/134 Ba barite-soln = δ 138/134 Ba barite − δ 138/134 Ba solution ) increasing in magnitude with increasing SI. Instead, the shifting precipitation mechanism(s) at high SI cause the fractionation factor to return to near-equilibrium values. This finding may be critical for understanding isotopic fractionation in localized extreme environments in nature, including for Ba isotopes during biomineralization. Published: 2026-07-01T17:41:20+00:00 Venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences Score: 0.567 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chuan Liu; Thomas L. Goût; Yandi Hu; Weiqiang Li; Shichao An; Zehao Zhou; Song Cai; Huazhang Zhao; Jinren Ni; James J. De Yoreo; Donald J. DePaolo

Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

DOI: 10.1073/pnas.2609659123

Relevance: 0.567 (flagship)

Isotopic fractionation is a unique indicator of the mechanisms of mineral precipitation from aqueous solutions, but existing theory does not account for nucleation effects or nonclassical growth mechanisms. Here, using barite (BaSO 4 ) as a model crystal, we provide an experimental measurement of isotope fractionation associated with nucleation. We isolated nucleation effects by precipitating crystals on an organic film, where cation enrichment creates a highly supersaturated microenvironment so nucleation dominates over crystal growth. In a single batch experiment, we retrieved nanocrystals (~1.5 nm) from the organic substrate and large microcrystals in bulk solution where growth dominates. The 138 Ba/ 134 Ba isotopic fractionation of −0.6 to −0.8‰ for the microcrystals match those expected for classical ion-by-ion growth from a moderately supersaturated solution, whereas the fractionation for the nucleation-dominated nanocrystals is about −0.1‰, falling within the reported equilibrium fractionation range between barite and aqueous Ba 2+ . These results demonstrate that cation isotopic fractionation in sparingly soluble salts like barite and calcite is not a single-valued function of saturation indices (SI) and precipitation rate as predicted with current theory, with the fractionation factor (∆ 138/134 Ba barite-soln = δ 138/134 Ba barite − δ 138/134 Ba solution ) increasing in magnitude with increasing SI. Instead, the shifting precipitation mechanism(s) at high SI cause the fractionation factor to return to near-equilibrium values. This finding may be critical for understanding isotopic fractionation in localized extreme environments in nature, including for Ba isotopes during biomineralization.

item.title: Urokodia sheds light on the origin of chelicerae and book gills of Chelicerata
item.dc:creator: Yu Liu
Lorenzo Lustri
Huijuan Mai
Mark Williams
Liming Liu
Yani Tang
Thomas H. P. Harvey
Xianguang Hou
item.description: Chelicerates are a diverse group of terrestrial and aquatic arthropods, yet their origin and early evolution remain debated1–6. Among different hypotheses7–11, one proposes that the front appendages, known as chelicerae, of chelicerates evolved from the short great appendages (SGAs) of Cambrian megacheirans12–16 and that book gills originated from their trunk limbs7. Although taxa such as Mollisonia plenovenatrix7 and Megachelicerax cousteaui16 provide important clues to the early evolution of chelicerates, the morphological transition from megacheiran-like appendages to true chelicerae and book gills remains unresolved. Here we use X-ray microtomography to reveal the three-dimensional anatomy of Urokodia aequalis, an early Cambrian euarthropod from the Chengjiang biota of China. Urokodia has a seven-segmented head with a sclerotized hypostome, pincer-like SGAs and biramous trunk appendages with overlapping exite flaps. Its pincer-like SGAs represent a bridge structure between the appearance of multisegmented SGAs and true chelicerae, and its trunk appendages support a megacheiran origin of book gills. Phylogenetic analyses consistently confirm the monophyly of Cheliceromorpha17, with Urokodia being the earliest-branching upper stem-group chelicerate that links lower stem-group megacheirans to crownward forms such as Mollisonia and Megachelicerax. Microtomography reconstructions and phylogenetic analyses of Urokodia aequalis from the Chengjiang biota of China provide an updated interpretation of the evolution of anatomical structures in chelicerates. Published: 2026-07-01T15:48:58+00:00 Venue: Nature Score: 0.491 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Liu; Lorenzo Lustri; Huijuan Mai; Mark Williams; Liming Liu; Yani Tang; Thomas H. P. Harvey; Xianguang Hou

Journal: Nature

DOI: 10.1038/s41586-026-10713-2

Relevance: 0.491 (flagship)

Chelicerates are a diverse group of terrestrial and aquatic arthropods, yet their origin and early evolution remain debated1–6. Among different hypotheses7–11, one proposes that the front appendages, known as chelicerae, of chelicerates evolved from the short great appendages (SGAs) of Cambrian megacheirans12–16 and that book gills originated from their trunk limbs7. Although taxa such as Mollisonia plenovenatrix7 and Megachelicerax cousteaui16 provide important clues to the early evolution of chelicerates, the morphological transition from megacheiran-like appendages to true chelicerae and book gills remains unresolved. Here we use X-ray microtomography to reveal the three-dimensional anatomy of Urokodia aequalis, an early Cambrian euarthropod from the Chengjiang biota of China. Urokodia has a seven-segmented head with a sclerotized hypostome, pincer-like SGAs and biramous trunk appendages with overlapping exite flaps. Its pincer-like SGAs represent a bridge structure between the appearance of multisegmented SGAs and true chelicerae, and its trunk appendages support a megacheiran origin of book gills. Phylogenetic analyses consistently confirm the monophyly of Cheliceromorpha17, with Urokodia being the earliest-branching upper stem-group chelicerate that links lower stem-group megacheirans to crownward forms such as Mollisonia and Megachelicerax. Microtomography reconstructions and phylogenetic analyses of Urokodia aequalis from the Chengjiang biota of China provide an updated interpretation of the evolution of anatomical structures in chelicerates.

item.title: Hadean bridgmanite in the source of a present-day ocean island
item.dc:creator: Claudine Israel
Catherine Chauvel
Edward Inglis
Hui Chen
Cécile Hébert
James Badro
item.description: Abstract Constraining the complex dynamics of the inner Earth unites research efforts across several scientific disciplines, including geochemistry, geophysics and geodynamics. Seismological and geodynamic studies offer insights into the present state of the mantle structure, whereas geochemical approaches characterize its chemical and isotopic heterogeneities 1 , shedding light on the complexity of its evolution. One key challenge is determining the age and origin of its chemical heterogeneities. Here we present new high-precision Nd isotopic measurements in present-day volcanism that identify heterogeneities dating back to the Earth’s earliest history. We report significantly positive 142 Nd anomalies in lavas from the submarine Fani Maoré volcano in the Comoros archipelago. These anomalies require the preservation, in the mantle, of material depleted in light rare-earth elements (REE) and formed within the first 100 million years (Myr) of Earth’s history. We suggest that this material is mainly composed of bridgmanite that crystallized from an early Earth magma ocean. This Hadean bridgmanite may be more widespread in the present-day mantle than previously expected, raising new questions about its survival over billions of years of plate tectonics and vigorous mantle convection. Published: 2026-07-01T15:31:05+00:00 Venue: Nature Score: 0.682 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Claudine Israel; Catherine Chauvel; Edward Inglis; Hui Chen; Cécile Hébert; James Badro

Journal: Nature

DOI: 10.1038/s41586-026-10719-w

Relevance: 0.682 (flagship)

Abstract Constraining the complex dynamics of the inner Earth unites research efforts across several scientific disciplines, including geochemistry, geophysics and geodynamics. Seismological and geodynamic studies offer insights into the present state of the mantle structure, whereas geochemical approaches characterize its chemical and isotopic heterogeneities 1 , shedding light on the complexity of its evolution. One key challenge is determining the age and origin of its chemical heterogeneities. Here we present new high-precision Nd isotopic measurements in present-day volcanism that identify heterogeneities dating back to the Earth’s earliest history. We report significantly positive 142 Nd anomalies in lavas from the submarine Fani Maoré volcano in the Comoros archipelago. These anomalies require the preservation, in the mantle, of material depleted in light rare-earth elements (REE) and formed within the first 100 million years (Myr) of Earth’s history. We suggest that this material is mainly composed of bridgmanite that crystallized from an early Earth magma ocean. This Hadean bridgmanite may be more widespread in the present-day mantle than previously expected, raising new questions about its survival over billions of years of plate tectonics and vigorous mantle convection.

item.title: Late Eocene to Oligocene intracontinental deformation and growth of the northwestern Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Zhiyuan He
He Wang
Shida Song
Zhenhong Wang
Zhongyu Xiong
Xiaoming Shen
Stijn Glorie
Yong Liang
Linglin Zhong
Johan De Grave
item.description: Abstract The timing and mechanisms of deformation along the northwestern Tibetan Plateau remain poorly constrained. Here we present new low-temperature thermochronological data from the western Songpan-Ganzi terrane, a key yet understudied region of the plateau interior. Apatite fission track and zircon and apatite (U-Th)/He ages cluster in the late Eocene-Oligocene, and thermal history modeling indicates moderate to rapid cooling and exhumation during this interval, followed by limited post-Oligocene cooling. These results suggest that deformation and exhumation were primarily concentrated in the late Eocene-Oligocene, with subsequent limited cooling and exhumation across the high-elevation, low-relief plateau interior. In contrast, younger cooling ages from the West Kunlun Mountains indicate that Neogene deformation and exhumation were localized along the plateau margin. Together, these observations highlight strongly diachronous deformation histories across northern Tibetan Plateau and provide new constraints on the spatiotemporal evolution of plateau development. Published: 2026-07-01T14:15:46+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.589 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhiyuan He; He Wang; Shida Song; Zhenhong Wang; Zhongyu Xiong; Xiaoming Shen; Stijn Glorie; Yong Liang; Linglin Zhong; Johan De Grave

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03776-6

Relevance: 0.589 (flagship)

Abstract The timing and mechanisms of deformation along the northwestern Tibetan Plateau remain poorly constrained. Here we present new low-temperature thermochronological data from the western Songpan-Ganzi terrane, a key yet understudied region of the plateau interior. Apatite fission track and zircon and apatite (U-Th)/He ages cluster in the late Eocene-Oligocene, and thermal history modeling indicates moderate to rapid cooling and exhumation during this interval, followed by limited post-Oligocene cooling. These results suggest that deformation and exhumation were primarily concentrated in the late Eocene-Oligocene, with subsequent limited cooling and exhumation across the high-elevation, low-relief plateau interior. In contrast, younger cooling ages from the West Kunlun Mountains indicate that Neogene deformation and exhumation were localized along the plateau margin. Together, these observations highlight strongly diachronous deformation histories across northern Tibetan Plateau and provide new constraints on the spatiotemporal evolution of plateau development.

item.title: Linking plates to plumes in the Cretaceous Pacific
item.dc:creator: Joanne Whittaker
item.description: Two independent studies imply that a remarkable burst of voluminous volcanism on the Pacific plate during the Cretaceous is attributable to coupled plate–mantle dynamics. Two independent studies imply that a remarkable burst of voluminous volcanism on the Pacific plate during the Cretaceous is attributable to coupled plate–mantle dynamics. Together, their findings show how enhanced subduction, mantle heterogeneity, and plate tectonic boundaries collectively controlled the timing and expression of these oceanic large igneous provinces.

item.title: Svalbard-barents ice sheet instability enhanced delivery of reactive iron to the ocean
item.dc:creator: Allyson Tessin
Christian März
Johan C. Faust
Philipp Böning
Matthias Forwick
Rachael Gray
Jens Matthiessen
Anke Neumann
Matthew O’Regan
Bernhard Schnetger
Christoph Vogt
item.description: Abstract Iron (Fe) plays a key role in marine environments as a bio-limiting nutrient and a control on sedimentary burial of carbon and other nutrients. Sensitive glacial systems provide an important delivery mechanism for Fe to the ocean, but it is unknown how these glacial sources will change as ice retreats. Sedimentary records from the Yermak Plateau, proximal to the Svalbard-Barents Ice Sheet provide an opportunity to investigate how past glacial instability may have affected bioavailable iron delivery. Here we find that over the past 135 ka, pulsed reactive Fe export to the Yermak Plateau occurred at least 5 times, during intervals of ice sheet retreat or advance. Fe-rich layers preserve high contents of reactive Fe phases, some of which were likely bioavailable at the time of deposition. Glacial Fe from Svalbard may have been delivered either directly by meltwater plumes or through remobilization of Fe deposited in fjord and shelf sediments. The delivery of reactive Fe hundreds of kilometers offshore during these events indicates that ice sheet instability in modern settings could perturb high latitude nutrient cycles. Published: 2026-07-01T05:02:03+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.502 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Allyson Tessin; Christian März; Johan C. Faust; Philipp Böning; Matthias Forwick; Rachael Gray; Jens Matthiessen; Anke Neumann; Matthew O’Regan; Bernhard Schnetger; Christoph Vogt

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-75133-2

Relevance: 0.502 (flagship)

Abstract Iron (Fe) plays a key role in marine environments as a bio-limiting nutrient and a control on sedimentary burial of carbon and other nutrients. Sensitive glacial systems provide an important delivery mechanism for Fe to the ocean, but it is unknown how these glacial sources will change as ice retreats. Sedimentary records from the Yermak Plateau, proximal to the Svalbard-Barents Ice Sheet provide an opportunity to investigate how past glacial instability may have affected bioavailable iron delivery. Here we find that over the past 135 ka, pulsed reactive Fe export to the Yermak Plateau occurred at least 5 times, during intervals of ice sheet retreat or advance. Fe-rich layers preserve high contents of reactive Fe phases, some of which were likely bioavailable at the time of deposition. Glacial Fe from Svalbard may have been delivered either directly by meltwater plumes or through remobilization of Fe deposited in fjord and shelf sediments. The delivery of reactive Fe hundreds of kilometers offshore during these events indicates that ice sheet instability in modern settings could perturb high latitude nutrient cycles.

item.title: Plate tectonic history and ocean oxygenation shaping biogeography of hydrothermal bacterial community
item.dc:creator: Weiguo Hou
Hailiang Dong
Hongyu Chen
Hanhui Liu
Yidi Zhang
Fangru Li
Xiqiu Han
Shang Wang
item.description: The influence of plate tectonics on microbial distributions remains poorly understood. Here, we demonstrate that the global biogeography of hydrothermal vent-endemic chemoautotrophic microbiota is structured by the tectonic history of the global major oceans. These microbiota, particularly anaerobic ones, are significantly more abundant in early-origin Pacific, Arctic, and Mediterranean oceans, whereas they are notably scarce in late-formed Atlantic and Indian Oceans. This pattern was attributed to the timing of ocean formation and its interplay with global redox evolution. Fully oxygenated conditions in the Phanerozoic during the formation of the latter two oceans imposed a dual-dispersal barrier among oceans: toxicity of molecular oxygen to anaerobes and depletion of energy sources (especially reduced chemicals) for aerobes, but such a barrier didn’t exist before the Phanerozoic, when the former three oceans started. These results integrate microbial biogeography into a geodynamic framework, revealing that even microbial life is subject to planetary-scale geological constraints. The links between deep-time geologic processes and microbial distribution remain poorly understood. This paper reveals that tectonic and geochemical evolution shapes the global biogeography of hydrothermal bacteria by limiting their dispersal to the oceans formed after oxygenation. Published: 2026-06-29T15:26:07+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.583 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Weiguo Hou; Hailiang Dong; Hongyu Chen; Hanhui Liu; Yidi Zhang; Fangru Li; Xiqiu Han; Shang Wang

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-74853-9

Relevance: 0.583 (flagship)

The influence of plate tectonics on microbial distributions remains poorly understood. Here, we demonstrate that the global biogeography of hydrothermal vent-endemic chemoautotrophic microbiota is structured by the tectonic history of the global major oceans. These microbiota, particularly anaerobic ones, are significantly more abundant in early-origin Pacific, Arctic, and Mediterranean oceans, whereas they are notably scarce in late-formed Atlantic and Indian Oceans. This pattern was attributed to the timing of ocean formation and its interplay with global redox evolution. Fully oxygenated conditions in the Phanerozoic during the formation of the latter two oceans imposed a dual-dispersal barrier among oceans: toxicity of molecular oxygen to anaerobes and depletion of energy sources (especially reduced chemicals) for aerobes, but such a barrier didn’t exist before the Phanerozoic, when the former three oceans started. These results integrate microbial biogeography into a geodynamic framework, revealing that even microbial life is subject to planetary-scale geological constraints. The links between deep-time geologic processes and microbial distribution remain poorly understood. This paper reveals that tectonic and geochemical evolution shapes the global biogeography of hydrothermal bacteria by limiting their dispersal to the oceans formed after oxygenation.

item.title: The deep link between intraplate volcanism and plate tectonics
item.dc:creator: Garrett Ito
item.description: Two independent studies suggest that thousands of submarine volcanoes in the Pacific Ocean and offshore Africa are likely to have resulted from two high-temperature structures in the lower mantle, whose location and morphology are influenced by plate subduction. Together, their findings imply many forms of intraplate volcanism are tied to plate tectonics through the deep mantle. Published: 2026-06-29T09:02:02+00:00 Venue: Nature Geoscience Score: 0.613 (flagship)

Two independent studies suggest that thousands of submarine volcanoes in the Pacific Ocean and offshore Africa are likely to have resulted from two high-temperature structures in the lower mantle, whose location and morphology are influenced by plate subduction. Together, their findings imply many forms of intraplate volcanism are tied to plate tectonics through the deep mantle.

item.title: A native sulfur deposit in Gale crater, Mars
item.dc:creator: Scott J. VanBommel
Jeff A. Berger
Penelope L. King
William E. Dietrich
Ralf Gellert
Lucy M. Thompson
Ashwin R. Vasavada
Alexander B. Bryk
Edwin S. Kite
Joanna V. Clark
Aster C. Cowart
Rebecca M. E. Williams
Sarah L. Simpson
Heather B. Franz
Catherine D. O’Connell-Cooper
Michael A. McCraig
Abigail A. Fraeman
John R. Christian
Abigail L. Knight
Nicholas I. Boyd
Deirdra M. Fey
Benton C. Clark
Christopher H. House
item.description: Martian rocks are known to contain sulfur-bearing species, including sulfates and sulfides. These compounds record a sulfur cycle that operated over Mars’ geological evolution. We used the Curiosity rover to investigate a deposit of light-toned stones in Gediz Vallis, within Gale crater on Mars. We find that the stones are composed of native sulfur. The sulfur deposit appears to have formed in place, within a sinuous entrenched canyon cut into the floor of Gediz Vallis. The presence of native sulfur implies that a sulfur enrichment pathway involving buoyant subsurface fluids operated on ancient Mars. We propose that the primary source of this sulfur was magmatic vapor, which cooled in the near subsurface cryosphere and was released by decompression during the erosion of Gediz Vallis. Published: 2026-06-29T00:00:27+00:00 Venue: Science Score: 0.659 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Scott J. VanBommel; Jeff A. Berger; Penelope L. King; William E. Dietrich; Ralf Gellert; Lucy M. Thompson; Ashwin R. Vasavada; Alexander B. Bryk; Edwin S. Kite; Joanna V. Clark; Aster C. Cowart; Rebecca M. E. Williams; Sarah L. Simpson; Heather B. Franz; Catherine D. O’Connell-Cooper; Michael A. McCraig; Abigail A. Fraeman; John R. Christian; Abigail L. Knight; Nicholas I. Boyd; Deirdra M. Fey; Benton C. Clark; Christopher H. House

Journal: Science

DOI: 10.1126/science.adu5501

Relevance: 0.659 (flagship)

Martian rocks are known to contain sulfur-bearing species, including sulfates and sulfides. These compounds record a sulfur cycle that operated over Mars’ geological evolution. We used the Curiosity rover to investigate a deposit of light-toned stones in Gediz Vallis, within Gale crater on Mars. We find that the stones are composed of native sulfur. The sulfur deposit appears to have formed in place, within a sinuous entrenched canyon cut into the floor of Gediz Vallis. The presence of native sulfur implies that a sulfur enrichment pathway involving buoyant subsurface fluids operated on ancient Mars. We propose that the primary source of this sulfur was magmatic vapor, which cooled in the near subsurface cryosphere and was released by decompression during the erosion of Gediz Vallis.

item.title: Enhanced crystal-melt segregation within dynamic mush systems links silicic cumulates with caldera-forming eruptions
item.dc:creator: Lucas M. Lino
Francy R. Quiroz-Valle
Silvio R. F. Vlach
Olivier Bachmann
Dougal A. Jerram
Valdecir de A. Janasi
Mathias Hueck
Antomat Avelino Macêdo Filho
Miguel Â. S. Basei
item.description: Abstract Supported by the mush model, the origin of crystal-poor, high-silica rhyolitic magmas (≥ 75 SiO₂ wt.%) is commonly linked to melt extraction in shallow, crystalline mushes, yet their complementary cumulates remain elusive in the upper crust. Here we present the “dynamic mush model” for the Campo Alegre-Corupá system (Brazil), combining textural analysis and thermodynamic modeling to show that high-silica melts formed through upper-crustal fractionation, leaving a granitic residue and feeding a caldera-forming eruption. Syenites and melasyenites represent their silicic-mafic cumulates, the syenites being formed at relatively low crystallinities (~16-33 of bulk vol.%) after extraction of large amounts of interstitial melts (~47-80 of liquid vol.%). In contrast to static models (extraction window ~50-70 vol.%), our results indicate early melt segregation in a dynamic reservoir. Alkali-feldspar crystals were size-selectively and hydraulically sorted by upward melt flow; aggregates were then formed at low crystallinities, supposedly via synneusis, enabling rapid sinking, and were repacked by recharge. This process, enhanced by relatively high magma fluxes, efficiently separated crystals from melt, explaining the origin of large-volume eruptible magmas in the upper crust. Our findings redefine mush evolution while underscoring the role of flow-driven crystal sorting (elutriation) in caldera-forming systems. Published: 2026-06-27T22:04:06+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.580 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lucas M. Lino; Francy R. Quiroz-Valle; Silvio R. F. Vlach; Olivier Bachmann; Dougal A. Jerram; Valdecir de A. Janasi; Mathias Hueck; Antomat Avelino Macêdo Filho; Miguel Â. S. Basei

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-74812-4

Relevance: 0.580 (flagship)

Abstract Supported by the mush model, the origin of crystal-poor, high-silica rhyolitic magmas (≥ 75 SiO₂ wt.%) is commonly linked to melt extraction in shallow, crystalline mushes, yet their complementary cumulates remain elusive in the upper crust. Here we present the “dynamic mush model” for the Campo Alegre-Corupá system (Brazil), combining textural analysis and thermodynamic modeling to show that high-silica melts formed through upper-crustal fractionation, leaving a granitic residue and feeding a caldera-forming eruption. Syenites and melasyenites represent their silicic-mafic cumulates, the syenites being formed at relatively low crystallinities (~16-33 of bulk vol.%) after extraction of large amounts of interstitial melts (~47-80 of liquid vol.%). In contrast to static models (extraction window ~50-70 vol.%), our results indicate early melt segregation in a dynamic reservoir. Alkali-feldspar crystals were size-selectively and hydraulically sorted by upward melt flow; aggregates were then formed at low crystallinities, supposedly via synneusis, enabling rapid sinking, and were repacked by recharge. This process, enhanced by relatively high magma fluxes, efficiently separated crystals from melt, explaining the origin of large-volume eruptible magmas in the upper crust. Our findings redefine mush evolution while underscoring the role of flow-driven crystal sorting (elutriation) in caldera-forming systems.

item.title: Primary controls on mineralization scale in the Zhunuo-Beimulang porphyry copper ore cluster, Gangdese Arc, Tibet: Input of juvenile lower-crust melt and fluid exsolution
item.dc:creator: Zezhong Du
Song Wu
Qi Chen
Xiaofeng Yao
Xiaofeng Liu
Yayun Zhao
Kang Hua
item.description: The Gangdese porphyry copper belt (GPCB) hosts Miocene porphyry Cu deposits within broadly similar intrusive rocks but showing pronounced variations in mineralization scale. The Zhunuo-Beimulang ore cluster, at the westernmost part of the GPCB, records two mineralization stages: a ∼ 16 Ma stage forming small deposits and prospects (e.g., Wubaduolai and Cimabanshuo), and a ∼ 14 Ma stage producing the Zhunuo and Beimulang deposits. Both stages show adakite-like affinities and evolved under similarly oxidized and hydrous conditions, providing a basis for evaluating porphyry Cu endowment in the district and GPCB. In this study, whole-rock and in situ geochemical and isotopic analyses were conducted on ore-forming porphyries from both stages. Early-stage porphyries show Sr/Y of 35–77, εHf(t) of −6.9 to −2.1, δ18O of 7.1–8.2 ‰, and ΔFMQ of 4.13–5.82, whereas late-stage porphyries show higher Sr/Y of 51–139, εHf(t) of −3.2 to 0.03, δ18O of 5.5–8.4 ‰, and ΔFMQ of 4.32–6.01. Sm-Nd and Hf isotopes indicate derivation mainly from partial melting of Tibetan juvenile lower crust with minor metasomatized mantle input. A source-constrained magma mixing model indicates increasing juvenile lower-crust contributions from 33 to 50 % in the ∼ 16 Ma magmas to 48–70 % in the ∼ 14 Ma magmas. Apatite volatile data further indicate more extensive fluid exsolution in the ∼ 14 Ma systems. Collectively, although adakitic affinity, high oxygen fugacity, and high water content establish a favorable magmatic background, variations in juvenile lower-crust input and fluid exsolution efficiency may control Cu endowment in Miocene porphyry systems in the GPCB. Published: 2026-06-30T23:36:33+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.703 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zezhong Du; Song Wu; Qi Chen; Xiaofeng Yao; Xiaofeng Liu; Yayun Zhao; Kang Hua

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107204

Relevance: 0.703 (must_read)

The Gangdese porphyry copper belt (GPCB) hosts Miocene porphyry Cu deposits within broadly similar intrusive rocks but showing pronounced variations in mineralization scale. The Zhunuo-Beimulang ore cluster, at the westernmost part of the GPCB, records two mineralization stages: a ∼ 16 Ma stage forming small deposits and prospects (e.g., Wubaduolai and Cimabanshuo), and a ∼ 14 Ma stage producing the Zhunuo and Beimulang deposits. Both stages show adakite-like affinities and evolved under similarly oxidized and hydrous conditions, providing a basis for evaluating porphyry Cu endowment in the district and GPCB. In this study, whole-rock and in situ geochemical and isotopic analyses were conducted on ore-forming porphyries from both stages. Early-stage porphyries show Sr/Y of 35–77, εHf(t) of −6.9 to −2.1, δ18O of 7.1–8.2 ‰, and ΔFMQ of 4.13–5.82, whereas late-stage porphyries show higher Sr/Y of 51–139, εHf(t) of −3.2 to 0.03, δ18O of 5.5–8.4 ‰, and ΔFMQ of 4.32–6.01. Sm-Nd and Hf isotopes indicate derivation mainly from partial melting of Tibetan juvenile lower crust with minor metasomatized mantle input. A source-constrained magma mixing model indicates increasing juvenile lower-crust contributions from 33 to 50 % in the ∼ 16 Ma magmas to 48–70 % in the ∼ 14 Ma magmas. Apatite volatile data further indicate more extensive fluid exsolution in the ∼ 14 Ma systems. Collectively, although adakitic affinity, high oxygen fugacity, and high water content establish a favorable magmatic background, variations in juvenile lower-crust input and fluid exsolution efficiency may control Cu endowment in Miocene porphyry systems in the GPCB.

item.title: Ore-forming volatiles linked to mafic magma recharge in the Xiongcun porphyry Cu-Au system, southern Tibet: Evidence from zircon petrochronology and apatite inclusions
item.dc:creator: Xinghai Lang
Xuhui Wang
Stephanie Lohmeier
Juxing Tang
Yulin Deng
Robert Moritz
item.description: The formation of porphyry Cu deposits (PCD) is related to deep magmatic systems, which serve as sources for porphyry magmas and metal- and sulfur-bearing mineralizing fluids. However, the interactions among deep magmas, mid- to upper-crustal magma chambers, and shallow porphyritic intrusions that trigger PCD formation remain poorly understood. This study focuses on the Xiongcun porphyry Cu-Au district in the Gangdese belt of the southern Tibetan Plateau, which experienced two distinct mineralization events: an earlier event related to a hornblende quartz diorite porphyry (HQDP), followed by a later event with a quartz diorite porphyry (QDP). The magmatic and volatile evolution linked to the Xiongcun PCD formation is investigated based on zircon textures, in situ zircon trace element and U-Pb isotope data, and the geochemical compositions of zircon-hosted apatite inclusions. Zircon U-Pb geochronology and trace element compositions indicate that the mid- to upper-crustal magma chamber linked to the Xiongcun porphyry system experienced mafic magma recharge at ca. 174.9 ± 0.4 Ma, followed by the emplacement of the HQDP (173.2 ± 0.4 Ma) and the QDP (170.6 ± 0.5 Ma). Variations in zircon trace element concentrations from both porphyries indicate that magmatic evolution was primarily driven by amphibole-dominated fractional crystallization, reflecting a hydrous magmatic system. Zircon-hosted apatite inclusions reveal a pronounced increase in volatile concentrations, particularly sulfur, likely provided by mafic magma recharge rejuvenating the mid- to upper-crustal magma chamber. Compared with the earlier and low-grade HQDP, the younger and more fertile QDP has lower zircon Gd/Yb ratios, and higher Eu/Eu*, (Ce/Nd)/Yb ratios, sulfur concentrations, and apatite XF/XCl ratios. Therefore, the formation of the high-grade Au-rich PCD centered on QDP is attributed to a protracted magmatic evolution in the mid- to upper-crustal magma chamber. During this prolonged period, the QDP likely underwent multiple mafic magma recharges and extensive fractional crystallization, leading to increasing volatile concentration, particularly sulfur, ultimately reaching volatile saturation. This study highlights the critical role of mafic magma recharge in the formation of the Xiongcun PCD, as it supplied essential ore-forming volatiles, especially sulfur, to the mid- to upper-crustal magmatic system. Published: 2026-06-30T21:17:24+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.686 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xinghai Lang; Xuhui Wang; Stephanie Lohmeier; Juxing Tang; Yulin Deng; Robert Moritz

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38551.1

Relevance: 0.686 (must_read)

The formation of porphyry Cu deposits (PCD) is related to deep magmatic systems, which serve as sources for porphyry magmas and metal- and sulfur-bearing mineralizing fluids. However, the interactions among deep magmas, mid- to upper-crustal magma chambers, and shallow porphyritic intrusions that trigger PCD formation remain poorly understood. This study focuses on the Xiongcun porphyry Cu-Au district in the Gangdese belt of the southern Tibetan Plateau, which experienced two distinct mineralization events: an earlier event related to a hornblende quartz diorite porphyry (HQDP), followed by a later event with a quartz diorite porphyry (QDP). The magmatic and volatile evolution linked to the Xiongcun PCD formation is investigated based on zircon textures, in situ zircon trace element and U-Pb isotope data, and the geochemical compositions of zircon-hosted apatite inclusions. Zircon U-Pb geochronology and trace element compositions indicate that the mid- to upper-crustal magma chamber linked to the Xiongcun porphyry system experienced mafic magma recharge at ca. 174.9 ± 0.4 Ma, followed by the emplacement of the HQDP (173.2 ± 0.4 Ma) and the QDP (170.6 ± 0.5 Ma). Variations in zircon trace element concentrations from both porphyries indicate that magmatic evolution was primarily driven by amphibole-dominated fractional crystallization, reflecting a hydrous magmatic system. Zircon-hosted apatite inclusions reveal a pronounced increase in volatile concentrations, particularly sulfur, likely provided by mafic magma recharge rejuvenating the mid- to upper-crustal magma chamber. Compared with the earlier and low-grade HQDP, the younger and more fertile QDP has lower zircon Gd/Yb ratios, and higher Eu/Eu*, (Ce/Nd)/Yb ratios, sulfur concentrations, and apatite XF/XCl ratios. Therefore, the formation of the high-grade Au-rich PCD centered on QDP is attributed to a protracted magmatic evolution in the mid- to upper-crustal magma chamber. During this prolonged period, the QDP likely underwent multiple mafic magma recharges and extensive fractional crystallization, leading to increasing volatile concentration, particularly sulfur, ultimately reaching volatile saturation. This study highlights the critical role of mafic magma recharge in the formation of the Xiongcun PCD, as it supplied essential ore-forming volatiles, especially sulfur, to the mid- to upper-crustal magmatic system.

item.title: Oceanic subduction zones: Factory of abiogenic hydrogen and methane
item.dc:creator: Yuhang Lu
Jiaxin Zhang
Wenbo Xu
Guibin Zhang
Lifei Zhang
Renbiao Tao
item.description: Molecular hydrogen (H_2) and methane (CH_4) are among the most crucial volatiles in the Earth system, serving as key energy carriers for both geological and biological processes. Extensive studies have constrained the formation mechanisms, redox conditions, and global fluxes of abiotic H_2 and CH_4 at divergent plate boundaries, particularly at mid-ocean ridges where fluid-rock reactions such as serpentinization are well characterized. In contrast, subduction zones, the representative convergent boundaries, act as efficient “factories” for the abiotic formation of H_2 and CH_4, yet the underlying processes and fluxes remain far less constrained. Within the subducting slab, the decomposition of organic matter and a series of high-pressure water-rock interactions can produce both biogenic and abiogenic H_2 and CH_4 under varying redox conditions. This review integrates recent advances and highlights four depth-dependent mechanisms that govern the formation of abiotic H_2 and CH_4 in subduction settings: shallow, low-pressure serpentinization during slab initiation, intermediate forearc serpentinization at elevated pressures, deep metamorphic fluid re-equilibration, and ultra-deep mantle metasomatism under ultra-high-pressure conditions. A comparison of potential gas fluxes between divergent and convergent boundaries suggests that subduction zones may represent a previously underestimated source of abiotic H_2 and CH_4. The upward migration and release of these gases could significantly influence hydrocarbon seepage, deep biosphere activity, and the redox evolution of Earth’s volatile cycles, underscoring the need for integrated experimental, thermodynamic, and isotopic investigations to quantify these processes better. Published: 2026-06-27T03:35:41+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.668 (must_read)

Molecular hydrogen (H_2) and methane (CH_4) are among the most crucial volatiles in the Earth system, serving as key energy carriers for both geological and biological processes. Extensive studies have constrained the formation mechanisms, redox conditions, and global fluxes of abiotic H_2 and CH_4 at divergent plate boundaries, particularly at mid-ocean ridges where fluid-rock reactions such as serpentinization are well characterized. Within the subducting slab, the decomposition of organic matter and a series of high-pressure water-rock interactions can produce both biogenic and abiogenic H_2 and CH_4 under varying redox conditions. This review integrates recent advances and highlights four depth-dependent mechanisms that govern the formation of abiotic H_2 and CH_4 in subduction settings: shallow, low-pressure serpentinization during slab initiation, intermediate forearc serpentinization at elevated pressures, deep metamorphic fluid re-equilibration, and ultra-deep mantle metasomatism under ultra-high-pressure conditions. A comparison of potential gas fluxes between divergent and convergent boundaries suggests that subduction zones may represent a previously underestimated source of abiotic H_2 and CH_4. The upward migration and release of these gases could significantly influence hydrocarbon seepage, deep biosphere activity, and the redox evolution of Earth’s volatile cycles, underscoring the need for integrated experimental, thermodynamic, and isotopic investigations to quantify these processes better.

item.title: Stable Mg-Zn isotope compositions in Mangaia HIMU lavas as tracers for ancient recycled carbonate
item.dc:creator: Si-Yi Yang
Yongsheng He
Oliver Nebel
Jon D. Woodhead
Yang Wang (汪洋)
Ying Han
Shan Ke
item.description: Intraplate lavas with heavy Zn isotopic compositions commonly exhibit light Mg isotope signatures, a pattern interpreted as evidence for recycling of Mg-rich carbonate. We report high-precision Mg and Zn isotopic data for lavas from Mangaia, an extreme HIMU OIB end-member thought to be derived from a CO2-rich parental melt. The Mangaia samples display whole-rock δ26Mg values ranging from −0.76‰ to −0.17‰, while δ66Zn varies narrowly between +0.19‰ to +0.39‰. After excluding samples affected by post-eruption alteration processes, we constrain the primary δ26Mg of the Mangaia lavas to −0.241 ± 0.018‰ (2SE, N = 15), indistinguishable from composition of depleted upper mantle and mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORB). In contrast, the primary δ66Zn is +0.35 ± 0.01‰ (2SE, N = 15), significantly higher than MORB, indicating a unique Mg and Zn isotope composition for the Mangaia mantle source. The elevated δ66Zn can be explained by a contribution of subducted altered oceanic crust (AOC), but a major AOC component is ruled out by petrological evidence for a peridotite-dominated source beneath Mangaia. Binary mixing and melting models show that the HIMU peridotite source was metasomatized by ≤ 10% melt derived from AOC containing 5–10% magnesite. The light Mg isotope signature of magnesite is diluted during mixing with the slab-derived silicates and ambient peridotite, whereas its heavy Zn isotope composition is preserved due to the high Zn concentration in magnesite. The ‘decoupled’ Mg-Zn isotope systematics of Mangaia thus reflect an ancient, well-homogenized carbonate-recycling event. In contrast, the light δ26Mg and heavy δ66Zn observed in some other intraplate basalts are consistent with melts generated directly from carbonate-rich portions of young subducting slabs, prior to extensive dilution by peridotitic mantle. This distinction makes combined Mg-Zn isotope investigations of intraplate lavas a useful tool to distinguish between contrasting carbonate recycling regimes, such as low-flux versus high-flux carbonate-rich melt metasomatism in ancient, hot subduction zones versus young, cold ones. Published: 2026-07-03T14:46:37+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.665 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Si-Yi Yang; Yongsheng He; Oliver Nebel; Jon D. Woodhead; Yang Wang (汪洋); Ying Han; Shan Ke

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.06.039

Relevance: 0.665 (must_read)

Intraplate lavas with heavy Zn isotopic compositions commonly exhibit light Mg isotope signatures, a pattern interpreted as evidence for recycling of Mg-rich carbonate. We report high-precision Mg and Zn isotopic data for lavas from Mangaia, an extreme HIMU OIB end-member thought to be derived from a CO2-rich parental melt. The Mangaia samples display whole-rock δ26Mg values ranging from −0.76‰ to −0.17‰, while δ66Zn varies narrowly between +0.19‰ to +0.39‰. After excluding samples affected by post-eruption alteration processes, we constrain the primary δ26Mg of the Mangaia lavas to −0.241 ± 0.018‰ (2SE, N = 15), indistinguishable from composition of depleted upper mantle and mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORB). In contrast, the primary δ66Zn is +0.35 ± 0.01‰ (2SE, N = 15), significantly higher than MORB, indicating a unique Mg and Zn isotope composition for the Mangaia mantle source. The elevated δ66Zn can be explained by a contribution of subducted altered oceanic crust (AOC), but a major AOC component is ruled out by petrological evidence for a peridotite-dominated source beneath Mangaia. Binary mixing and melting models show that the HIMU peridotite source was metasomatized by ≤ 10% melt derived from AOC containing 5–10% magnesite. The light Mg isotope signature of magnesite is diluted during mixing with the slab-derived silicates and ambient peridotite, whereas its heavy Zn isotope composition is preserved due to the high Zn concentration in magnesite. The ‘decoupled’ Mg-Zn isotope systematics of Mangaia thus reflect an ancient, well-homogenized carbonate-recycling event. In contrast, the light δ26Mg and heavy δ66Zn observed in some other intraplate basalts are consistent with melts generated directly from carbonate-rich portions of young subducting slabs, prior to extensive dilution by peridotitic mantle. This distinction makes combined Mg-Zn isotope investigations of intraplate lavas a useful tool to distinguish between contrasting carbonate recycling regimes, such as low-flux versus high-flux carbonate-rich melt metasomatism in ancient, hot subduction zones versus young, cold ones.

item.title: Rubidium isotopes reveal no sub-arc melting of altered oceanic crusts in a cold subduction zone
item.dc:creator: Zi-Qiang Zhong
Ding-Sheng Jiang
Saskia Erdmann
Yi-Xiang Chen
Xia Hu
Zheng Xu
Wei Zhang
Jia-Ru Sheng
Wang-Ye Li
Fang Huang
item.description: The extent to which partial melting of subducting slabs contributes to arc magmatism beneath sub-arc regions in cold subduction zones remains highly debated. Traditional models emphasize that altered oceanic crust (AOC) undergoes dehydration, while subducted sediments undergo partial melting. However, an increasing number of studies have argued that partial melting of AOC may also be required to account for the trace-element budgets of arc lavas, particularly their elevated Sr concentrations. To investigate whether AOC undergoes partial melting beneath sub-arc regions, we report Rb isotope (δ87Rb) data for the Cretaceous arc basalts and back-arc basalts associated with the cold subduction zone of the Izanagi plate in Eastern China. The δ87Rb values of the basalts show clear variation across the subduction zone, increasing from −0.31 ± 0.45‰ (2SD, N = 9) for the arc basalts to −0.19 ± 0.07‰ (2SD, N = 22) for the back-arc basalts. This across-arc variation in δ87Rb indicates that while partial melting of subducted sediments occurs across the sub-arc and back-arc regions, partial melting of AOC is only evident in the back-arc region, not beneath the sub-arc region. Moreover, negative correlations between δ87Rb and Sr concentrations strongly suggest that the elevated Sr concentrations (385–913 μg/g) of the arc basalts resulted from the subduction input from sediments, rather than AOC melts as has recently been proposed for global data. Similar compositional relations are observed in arc lavas from other cold subduction zones (Izu-Bonin-Mariana and Tonga-Kermadec), which typically exhibit a negative correlation between Sr/Y and 143Nd/144Nd or Nb/U ratios. Together with recent δ87Rb data from the Mariana arc, our results indicate that in cold subduction zones, AOC may not necessarily undergo partial melting beneath the sub-arc region—even though subducted sediments do. Partial melts of metasomatic pyroxenites formed by interaction between sediment melts and the sub-arc mantle peridotites can carry a significant budget of trace elements, including Sr, contributing to arc magmas. The lack of partial melting in AOC beneath sub-arc regions permits the deep transport of water, carbon, and trace elements, with significant implications for mantle heterogeneity, carbon flux imbalance, and global geochemical cycling. Published: 2026-07-03T23:01:35+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.645 (must_read)

Authors: Zi-Qiang Zhong; Ding-Sheng Jiang; Saskia Erdmann; Yi-Xiang Chen; Xia Hu; Zheng Xu; Wei Zhang; Jia-Ru Sheng; Wang-Ye Li; Fang Huang

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.002

Relevance: 0.645 (must_read)

The extent to which partial melting of subducting slabs contributes to arc magmatism beneath sub-arc regions in cold subduction zones remains highly debated. Traditional models emphasize that altered oceanic crust (AOC) undergoes dehydration, while subducted sediments undergo partial melting. However, an increasing number of studies have argued that partial melting of AOC may also be required to account for the trace-element budgets of arc lavas, particularly their elevated Sr concentrations. To investigate whether AOC undergoes partial melting beneath sub-arc regions, we report Rb isotope (δ87Rb) data for the Cretaceous arc basalts and back-arc basalts associated with the cold subduction zone of the Izanagi plate in Eastern China. The δ87Rb values of the basalts show clear variation across the subduction zone, increasing from −0.31 ± 0.45‰ (2SD, N = 9) for the arc basalts to −0.19 ± 0.07‰ (2SD, N = 22) for the back-arc basalts. This across-arc variation in δ87Rb indicates that while partial melting of subducted sediments occurs across the sub-arc and back-arc regions, partial melting of AOC is only evident in the back-arc region, not beneath the sub-arc region. Moreover, negative correlations between δ87Rb and Sr concentrations strongly suggest that the elevated Sr concentrations (385–913 μg/g) of the arc basalts resulted from the subduction input from sediments, rather than AOC melts as has recently been proposed for global data. Similar compositional relations are observed in arc lavas from other cold subduction zones (Izu-Bonin-Mariana and Tonga-Kermadec), which typically exhibit a negative correlation between Sr/Y and 143Nd/144Nd or Nb/U ratios. Together with recent δ87Rb data from the Mariana arc, our results indicate that in cold subduction zones, AOC may not necessarily undergo partial melting beneath the sub-arc region—even though subducted sediments do. Partial melts of metasomatic pyroxenites formed by interaction between sediment melts and the sub-arc mantle peridotites can carry a significant budget of trace elements, including Sr, contributing to arc magmas. The lack of partial melting in AOC beneath sub-arc regions permits the deep transport of water, carbon, and trace elements, with significant implications for mantle heterogeneity, carbon flux imbalance, and global geochemical cycling.

item.title: Seamount subduction drives cyclicity in continental arc magmatism: A record from southeastern Lhasa terrane
item.dc:creator: Yuan-Jun Mai
Wen-Guang Yang
Xin Jin
Yao Zhong
Li-Dong Zhu
item.description: Continental arc magmatism is characterized by episodic flare-ups and lulls, a rhythmicity that profoundly influences crustal recycling, yet the driving mechanisms remain unresolved. Here, through an integrated study of the Xionglai high-Mg andesites in southern Tibet, we propose that seamount subduction played a key role in regulating this process. Petrological, geochemical, and Sr-Nd-Hf isotopic data show the Xionglai HMA (199–196 Ma) has enriched isotopic signatures and abundant inherited zircons with forearc-derived age spectra. Critically, these inherited zircons share comparable Raman spectral characteristics and radiation damage levels with syn-magmatic zircons, indicating a shared thermal history that precludes shallow crustal assimilation. These features are consistent with melting of mélange diapirs formed from subducted sediments and mantle wedge. We temporally correlate two distinct magmatic flare-ups (ca. 280–260 Ma and ca. 210–185 Ma) with two seamount subduction events, as recorded by seamount fragments and eclogite-facies metamorphism. This correlation supports a model in which buoyant seamount subduction enhances erosion, delivering sediments to form mélange diapirs whose melting triggers flare-ups. Our findings highlight seamount subduction as a plausible mechanism generating cyclic continental arc magmatism by modulating sediment input and slab dynamics, thereby contributing to the cyclicity of continental arc magmatism. Published: 2026-06-27T15:15:34+00:00 Venue: Gondwana Research Score: 0.637 (must_read)

Continental arc magmatism is characterized by episodic flare-ups and lulls, a rhythmicity that profoundly influences crustal recycling, yet the driving mechanisms remain unresolved. Here, through an integrated study of the Xionglai high-Mg andesites in southern Tibet, we propose that seamount subduction played a key role in regulating this process. Petrological, geochemical, and Sr-Nd-Hf isotopic data show the Xionglai HMA (199–196 Ma) has enriched isotopic signatures and abundant inherited zircons with forearc-derived age spectra. Critically, these inherited zircons share comparable Raman spectral characteristics and radiation damage levels with syn-magmatic zircons, indicating a shared thermal history that precludes shallow crustal assimilation. These features are consistent with melting of mélange diapirs formed from subducted sediments and mantle wedge. We temporally correlate two distinct magmatic flare-ups (ca. 280–260 Ma and ca. 210–185 Ma) with two seamount subduction events, as recorded by seamount fragments and eclogite-facies metamorphism. This correlation supports a model in which buoyant seamount subduction enhances erosion, delivering sediments to form mélange diapirs whose melting triggers flare-ups. Our findings highlight seamount subduction as a plausible mechanism generating cyclic continental arc magmatism by modulating sediment input and slab dynamics, thereby contributing to the cyclicity of continental arc magmatism.

item.title: Genetic linkage between porphyry and structure-controlled gold mineralization: evidence from hydrothermal zircon and S isotopes at the Bada Deposit, Eastern Tibet
item.dc:creator: Zi Wang
Hesen Zhao
Xinyu Zhang
Wenhao Li
Tianhang Ren
Jinquan Chen
Fuwei Xie
item.description: Porphyry and related hydrothermal gold deposits constitute a major portion of global gold resources. However, the genetic relationship between porphyry systems and spatially associated structure-controlled gold mineralization remains contentious, particularly when cross-cutting relationships obscure temporal links. This study investigates the Bada Cu–Au deposit in the eastern Tethyan belt, western China, where late-stage, structure-controlled gold veins overprint porphyry intrusions. NW- and NE-trending faults, together with associated joints and fractures, control quartz-sulfide-sericite-argillic alteration and Au mineralization. U–Pb dating of hydrothermal zircon grains separated from mineralized fault gouge yields a Concordia age of 34.6 ± 0.5 Ma (n = 30), indistinguishable from the emplacement age of the Bada porphyry (∼35.0 Ma). These hydrothermal zircons exhibit higher Th/U ratios and Y content than magmatic zircons but display comparable rare earth element patterns and oxygen fugacity estimates (ΔFMQ = +0.5 to + 2.5). In situ LA-MC-ICP-MS sulfur isotope analyses reveal a systematic shift from magmatic sulfides in barren porphyry (δ34S ≈ 1.2‰) to hydrothermal sulfides in fault gouge (δ34S = 4.3–7.7‰), indicating sulfur isotope fractionation during fluid exsolution. These results demonstrate that structure-controlled gold mineralization at Bada represents a late-stage expression of the same porphyry system, driven by magmatic–hydrothermal fluids derived from a shared deep-seated magma source. Porphyry emplacement, structural reactivation, and subsequent Au mineralization occurred within a narrow temporal window (<1 Myr). The mineralized structures and associated Au mineralization are spatially fractal, secondary products of the regional Yulong metallogenic event driven by pull-apart basin tectonics. This model provides a viable framework for deciphering genetic relationships in other metallogenic belts exhibiting similar cross-cutting relationships. Published: 2026-06-27T23:00:12+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.633 (consider)

Porphyry and related hydrothermal gold deposits constitute a major portion of global gold resources. However, the genetic relationship between porphyry systems and spatially associated structure-controlled gold mineralization remains contentious, particularly when cross-cutting relationships obscure temporal links. This study investigates the Bada Cu–Au deposit in the eastern Tethyan belt, western China, where late-stage, structure-controlled gold veins overprint porphyry intrusions. This model provides a viable framework for deciphering genetic relationships in other metallogenic belts exhibiting similar cross-cutting relationships.

item.title: Occurrence and petrogenesis of diamondiferous lamprophyres: Insights into mantle metasomatism and diamond formation
item.dc:creator: Francesca Innocenzi
Maxwell C. Day
Fabrizio Nestola
Davide Novella
Mattia La Fortezza
Kristina Kublik
D. Graham Pearson
Martha G. Pamato
item.description: Lamprophyres are a rare rock-type that include different lithologies, classified mineralogically (abundance of mica, amphibole, feldspathoids) rather than geochemically (enrichment in alkalis and volatiles). Despite the attention that these rocks have attracted, due to their possible economic interest, their sources, petrogenesis and classification remain a matter of debate. One of the most intriguing aspects is the relationship of some lamprophyre sub-types with diamonds, especially because diamondiferous lamprophyres are the rarest diamond-bearing rock-types. The reason for this association and their potential genetic relationship remain poorly understood. Previous studies on lamprophyres (diamond-bearing and barren) are reviewed to improve our knowledge of peculiar mantle paragenesis, fertilization events, metasomatic agents and the relationship between lamprophyres and diamonds. Calc-alkaline lamprophyres (CALs) are often emplaced in subduction-related settings, where slab dehydration and crustal recycling can stabilize hydrous phases (amphibole/phlogopite). Low degree partial melting of shallow metasomatized sources may explain CALs petrogenesis. To reconcile this shallow formation depth with the presence of diamonds, we propose a lithospheric stacking model, that results in a highly transient geotherm during the initial phases of craton formation. Ultramatic lamprophyres (UMLs) mostly crop out along rifts and are related to a veined lithospheric and/or asthenospheric source. A direct genetic link between diamonds and UMLs can be excluded. Instead, the presence of diamondiferous UMLs relies on spatial and temporal connections (e.g., mantle domains enriched in C-H-phases). Lamprophyres are hostile magma for diamonds, due to their high fO2, H2O and CO2, along with a longer lag time and a lower volume of emplaced magma. Published: 2026-06-29T23:02:18+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.626 (consider)
