The concealed Duxiushan intrusion, located in the Anqing district of the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt in eastern China, is a newly recognized granite–pegmatite complex that offers critical insights into the late-stage evolution of highly fractionated granitic magmatism in this region. To investigate its petrogenesis and potential for critical metal enrichment, we employed a combination of monazite and apatite U–Pb dating (which circumvents the common problem of zircon metamictization in highly evolved systems), whole-rock geochemistry, Sr–Nd isotopes, and mineral chemistry. The obtained ages, ranging from approximately 130 to 124 Ma, indicate emplacement during Early Cretaceous lithospheric extension, linked to the rollback of the Paleo-Pacific Plate. The two-mica granite, muscovite granite, and pegmatite exhibit consistent mineralogical and geochemical trends, suggesting that they originated from a common crustal melt primarily derived from the partial melting of ancient metasedimentary rocks, as indicated by their enriched Sr-Nd isotopic compositions, with minimal mantle-derived contributions. Systematic enrichment in SiO2, Rb, and F, alongside depletion in Ba, Sr, and Eu, reflects significant fractional crystallization. Compositional analysis of mica and apatite reveals a decrease in oxygen fugacity and the evolution of F-dominated magmatic fluids under reduced conditions, creating favorable conditions for the retention of incompatible elements during magma evolution. LA–ICP–MS mapping identifies Rb concentrations exceeding 1500 ppm in pegmatitic muscovite, confirming significant mineral-scale Rb enrichment. These findings demonstrate that the concealed Duxiushan granite–pegmatite system represents a late-stage product of crustal anatexis during Early Cretaceous extension in the Middle-Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt.

item.title: Hydrogen and mantle plumes
item.dc:creator: Vladimir G. Trifonov
Sergey Yu. Sokolov
Mikhail A. Fedonkin
item.description: This paper analyzes geological, geophysical, and geochemical data, along with behavior of minerals under high temperatures and pressures, to assess a possible role of hydrogen and its compounds in formation and ascent of mantle plumes. Positive correlation between hydrogen and helium concentrations in the mantle enables the use of helium data to estimate hydrogen content in plumes. Three categories of plumes are examined: the African and Pacific superplumes, which represent ascending branches of whole-mantle convection; intra-mantle plumes, which rise from the upper part of the lower mantle; and upper mantle plumes, which are confined to the upper mantle and transition zone. The analysis reveals diversity of redox conditions across different mantle regions. In the lower mantle of superplumes, increased temperatures and, possibly, reducing conditions prevail. Hydrogen can exist there as hydrides and H2 molecules, partly dissolved in silicates. In descending branches of whole-mantle convection, the environment can become more oxidizing, and the hydrogen content decreases. In the transition zone, ringwoodite and wadsleyite can incorporate hydroxyl groups that form aqueous fluids that promote development of upper mantle plumes. In the upper crust, redox reactions generate additional hydrogen sources, associated, in particular, with the oxidation of divalent iron to trivalent. The presumed presence of hydrogen and/or water in mantle plumes reduces elastic properties of rocks and finally can increase their fluidity by weakening interatomic bonds and activating dislocation creep. This can facilitate faster plume ascent. Hydrogen emissions associated with plumes and rift zones may provide a promising energy source for industrial hydrogen production. Published: 2026-06-30T20:01:29+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.583 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vladimir G. Trifonov; Sergey Yu. Sokolov; Mikhail A. Fedonkin

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105608

Relevance: 0.583 (consider)

This paper analyzes geological, geophysical, and geochemical data, along with behavior of minerals under high temperatures and pressures, to assess a possible role of hydrogen and its compounds in formation and ascent of mantle plumes. Positive correlation between hydrogen and helium concentrations in the mantle enables the use of helium data to estimate hydrogen content in plumes. Three categories of plumes are examined: the African and Pacific superplumes, which represent ascending branches of whole-mantle convection; intra-mantle plumes, which rise from the upper part of the lower mantle; and upper mantle plumes, which are confined to the upper mantle and transition zone. The analysis reveals diversity of redox conditions across different mantle regions. In the lower mantle of superplumes, increased temperatures and, possibly, reducing conditions prevail. Hydrogen can exist there as hydrides and H2 molecules, partly dissolved in silicates. In descending branches of whole-mantle convection, the environment can become more oxidizing, and the hydrogen content decreases. In the transition zone, ringwoodite and wadsleyite can incorporate hydroxyl groups that form aqueous fluids that promote development of upper mantle plumes. In the upper crust, redox reactions generate additional hydrogen sources, associated, in particular, with the oxidation of divalent iron to trivalent. The presumed presence of hydrogen and/or water in mantle plumes reduces elastic properties of rocks and finally can increase their fluidity by weakening interatomic bonds and activating dislocation creep. This can facilitate faster plume ascent. Hydrogen emissions associated with plumes and rift zones may provide a promising energy source for industrial hydrogen production.

item.title: Detrital zircon reveals secular evolution of granite petrogenesis from early Earth geodynamics to supercontinent cycles
item.dc:creator: Mingde Lang
Zhaochong Zhang
Tom E. Sheldrake
Ruixuan Zhang
Zhiguo Cheng
Yining Shi
item.description: The secular evolution of granitoid magmatism provides a key to understanding the transition from early Earth’s geodynamic regimes to modern plate tectonics and the co-evolution of continental crust with supercontinent cycles. Detrital zircons preserve a continuous record of this evolution, but previous provenance classifications have been limited to a binary I-S-type scheme, overlooking A-type, M−type, and non-granitic sources. Here we present a two-step machine-learning classifier (Random Forest, 93.2 % overall accuracy), trained on 8,101 magmatic zircons with known host-rock context, that discriminates among I-, S-, A-, M−type granitoid and non-granitic sources based on 18 trace-element features. Applied to 11,591 detrital zircons older than 500 Ma, the classifier reveals first-order Precambrian trends in granite petrogenesis and their links to supercontinent evolution. S-type zircons appear as early as ∼ 4.3 Ga, supporting Hadean liquid water and supracrustal recycling. M−type zircons are first recognized after ∼ 3.5 Ga and increase markedly beginning just before ∼ 2.0 Ga, suggesting a progressive involvement of mantle-derived magmas consistent with a transition to more widespread horizontal tectonics. I-type zircon abundances display declines coincident with the Great Oxidation Event and Neoproterozoic Oxidation Event, motivating a testable hypothesis that links deep reducing-gas fluxes, atmospheric O2 sinks, and redox conditions unfavorable for typical oxidized I-type granitoid magmatism. A-type zircon enrichment intervals, especially around ∼ 2.7–2.5 Ga, ∼1.85–1.75 Ga, and ∼ 1.25–1.10 Ga, broadly correspond to lithospheric thinning and post-orogenic to intraplate extension, suggesting that A-type zircon abundance records lithospheric thermal reorganization, pointing to gravitational delamination as a dominant control. By explicitly including all granitoid types and non-granitic sources and framing the results within the context of supercontinent cycles, our approach provides a more complete and quantitative framework for reading Earth’s deep-time history from detrital zircon archives, offering new insights into the coupled evolution of the continental lithosphere, surface environments, and global geodynamics. Published: 2026-06-27T23:04:54+00:00 Venue: Gondwana Research Score: 0.576 (consider)

The secular evolution of granitoid magmatism provides a key to understanding the transition from early Earth’s geodynamic regimes to modern plate tectonics and the co-evolution of continental crust with supercontinent cycles. Detrital zircons preserve a continuous record of this evolution, but previous provenance classifications have been limited to a binary I-S-type scheme, overlooking A-type, M−type, and non-granitic sources. Here we present a two-step machine-learning classifier (Random Forest, 93.2 % overall accuracy), trained on 8,101 magmatic zircons with known host-rock context, that discriminates among I-, S-, A-, M−type granitoid and non-granitic sources based on 18 trace-element features. Applied to 11,591 detrital zircons older than 500 Ma, the classifier reveals first-order Precambrian trends in granite petrogenesis and their links to supercontinent evolution. S-type zircons appear as early as ∼ 4.3 Ga, supporting Hadean liquid water and supracrustal recycling. M−type zircons are first recognized after ∼ 3.5 Ga and increase markedly beginning just before ∼ 2.0 Ga, suggesting a progressive involvement of mantle-derived magmas consistent with a transition to more widespread horizontal tectonics. I-type zircon abundances display declines coincident with the Great Oxidation Event and Neoproterozoic Oxidation Event, motivating a testable hypothesis that links deep reducing-gas fluxes, atmospheric O2 sinks, and redox conditions unfavorable for typical oxidized I-type granitoid magmatism. A-type zircon enrichment intervals, especially around ∼ 2.7–2.5 Ga, ∼1.85–1.75 Ga, and ∼ 1.25–1.10 Ga, broadly correspond to lithospheric thinning and post-orogenic to intraplate extension, suggesting that A-type zircon abundance records lithospheric thermal reorganization, pointing to gravitational delamination as a dominant control. By explicitly including all granitoid types and non-granitic sources and framing the results within the context of supercontinent cycles, our approach provides a more complete and quantitative framework for reading Earth’s deep-time history from detrital zircon archives, offering new insights into the coupled evolution of the continental lithosphere, surface environments, and global geodynamics.

item.title: Mixed paragenesis diamond inclusions form during metasomatic fluid evolution
item.dc:creator: S. Mikhail
M. Rinaldi
D.A. Sverjensky
item.description: Mineral inclusions in diamond provide the context for diamond formation, yet some diamonds contain disequilibrium ‘mixed paragenesis’ mineral inclusions. Mixed paragenesis diamonds are inferred to somehow record growth of a single diamond in both mafic eclogitic and peridotitic substrates. Here we use thermodynamic modelling with the Extended Deep Earth Water (DEW) model to show that mixed paragenesis mineral inclusions can form during a single episode of diamond growth via fluid-rock metasomatism in the lithospheric mantle. Simulations of fluid infiltration and reactions predict sequential mineral precipitation, with early eclogitic minerals (omphacite and garnet) followed by more peridotitic minerals (e.g., olivine and Mg-rich garnet) during continued diamond growth. Concurrently, modelled fluids evolve from silicic toward carbonatitic compositions and reproduce the compositional range of high density fluids observed in fibrous diamonds. These results demonstrate that mixed paragenesis inclusions and the diversity of diamond-forming fluids can arise from progressive metasomatic reactions during a single diamond-forming event, rather than requiring a diamond to grow first in eclogite and then in peridotite. Published: 2026-07-01T09:16:53+00:00 Venue: Geochemical Perspectives Letters Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: S. Mikhail; M. Rinaldi; D.A. Sverjensky

Journal: Geochemical Perspectives Letters

DOI: 10.7185/geochemlet.2625

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Mineral inclusions in diamond provide the context for diamond formation, yet some diamonds contain disequilibrium ‘mixed paragenesis’ mineral inclusions. Mixed paragenesis diamonds are inferred to somehow record growth of a single diamond in both mafic eclogitic and peridotitic substrates. Here we use thermodynamic modelling with the Extended Deep Earth Water (DEW) model to show that mixed paragenesis mineral inclusions can form during a single episode of diamond growth via fluid-rock metasomatism in the lithospheric mantle. Simulations of fluid infiltration and reactions predict sequential mineral precipitation, with early eclogitic minerals (omphacite and garnet) followed by more peridotitic minerals (e.g., olivine and Mg-rich garnet) during continued diamond growth. Concurrently, modelled fluids evolve from silicic toward carbonatitic compositions and reproduce the compositional range of high density fluids observed in fibrous diamonds. These results demonstrate that mixed paragenesis inclusions and the diversity of diamond-forming fluids can arise from progressive metasomatic reactions during a single diamond-forming event, rather than requiring a diamond to grow first in eclogite and then in peridotite.
